coredata.dc:title: Enhanced chemiluminescence by carbon dots for rapid detection of bisphenol A and nitrite

Long, Xiaoqin
Zhang, Xue
Mu, Junjie
Chen, Qiuxiong

Li, Ying
Xue, Hao
Song, Pengyang
Yang, Xian
Zheng, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel chemiluminescence (CL) sensor was successfully developed based on chlorine doped carbon dots (Cl/CDs) for the rapid determination of bisphenol A (BPA) and nitrite. The Cl/CDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal method, using ascorbic acid as the precursor and hydrochloric acid as the dopant. It was found that Cl/CDs significantly enhanced the CL intensity of the acid-KMnO4 system, while BPA and nitrite quenched the CL intensity of the Cl/CDs-sensitized acid-KMnO4 system. Under optimal conditions, BPA exhibited a linear detection range of 0.05–10 μM, with limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) of 0.86 nM and 2.8 nM, respectively. Nitrite showed a linear detection range of 0.7–100 μM, with LOD and LOQ of 22.5 nM and 75 nM, respectively. The CL sensor was successfully use to determine BPA in water samples and nitrite in pickles, ham and celery, with spike recovery rates of 96.3 %–104.8 % and 96.0 %–104.9 %, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chemiluminescence
Nitrite
Bisphenol A

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enhancement of the KMnO4 + HCHO system by Cl/CDs
Optimization of the experimental conditions for BPA and nitrite detection

Thanks for the financial support from the project \u2018Wildlife Resource Survey of Montenegro Nature Reserve in Wansheng\u2019.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of Cl/CDs at different magnifications, the right insets of (a) and (b) are histograms of the size distribution of Cl/CDs and lattice fringe image.

(a) Raman spectrum of Cl/CDs, (b) XRD spectrum of Cl/CDs, (c) FT-IR spectrum of Cl/CDs, (d)XPS survey scan spectrum (e)C 1 s, (f) O 1 s, (g) Cl 2p spectra of Cl/CDs, (h) UV–vis absorption spectra of the Cl/CDs. Inset: images of the Cl/CDs in daylight (left) and under 365 nm UV light (right), (i) FL spectra of the Cl/CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

CL signal of Cl/CDs-KMnO4 system with nitrite or other species, the concentration of all species were 5 μM (a), CL signal of Cl/CDs-KMnO4 system with BPA or other species, the concentration of all species were 1 μM (b), CL signal-times curves at different concentrations of nitrite (c) and BPA (d), linear relationship between CL intensity and nitrite (e) and BPA (f).
Detection limits and linear ranges of different methods for BPA and nitrite detection.
Effects of common interfering substances on the determination of BPA and nitrite.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of BPA and nitrite in real sample.

head.ce:title: Enhanced chemiluminescence by carbon dots for rapid detection of bisphenol A and nitrite

Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing 401331, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel chemiluminescence (CL) sensor was successfully developed based on chlorine doped carbon dots (Cl/CDs) for the rapid determination of bisphenol A (BPA) and nitrite. The Cl/CDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal method, using ascorbic acid as the precursor and hydrochloric acid as the dopant. It was found that Cl/CDs significantly enhanced the CL intensity of the acid-KMnO4 system, while BPA and nitrite quenched the CL intensity of the Cl/CDs-sensitized acid-KMnO4 system. Under optimal conditions, BPA exhibited a linear detection range of 0.05–10 μM, with limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) of 0.86 nM and 2.8 nM, respectively. Nitrite showed a linear detection range of 0.7–100 μM, with LOD and LOQ of 22.5 nM and 75 nM, respectively. The CL sensor was successfully use to determine BPA in water samples and nitrite in pickles, ham and celery, with spike recovery rates of 96.3 %–104.8 % and 96.0 %–104.9 %, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol A (BPA) is a monomer used in the synthesis of polycarbonate and epoxy resins,in addition to other plastics (Dhanjai et al., 2018). It is widely employed in food, and beverage storage and packaging materials, such as water bottles, baby bottles, and food packaging liners (BPA is also used to protectively coat metal cans to prevent contamination and extend product shelf life) (Metz, 2016). BPA has been linked to various health concerns, including breast and prostate cancer, menstrual irregularities, and endocrine disruption. Excessive BPA can result in metabolic disorders and potential carcinogenic effects, posing significant health risks (Facina et al., 2021). The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) stipulates that the maximum daily intake of BPA should not exceed 0.05 μg/mL (Santovito et al., 2018). Vast amounts of plastic waste containing BPA are produced annually, once these products reach landfills, BPA is released into the environment, especially surface water. Nitrite, which is often present as sodium nitrite, is an industrial salt derived from nitrous acid ions. It is commonly used in the food industry as a preservative and meat colouring agent (Govari & Pexara, 2018), playing a crucial role in producing cured meats, pickles, and canned foods. However, it is important to note that high levels of nitrites are hazardous to infants as they can cause methemoglobinemia (Johnson, 2019). Furthermore, when nitrites react with secondary or tertiary amines, carcinogenic nitrosamines are formed. Chronic intake of nitrites from processed meats may lead to colorectal cancer (Bouvard et al., 2015). According to large prospective cohort studies and epidemiological analyses in the United States and the European Union, chronic intake of higher quantities of red meats, especially processed meats, is associated with higher rates of mortality, colorectal cancer, type 2 diabetes, and heart disease in both men and women (Battaglia Richi et al., 2015). Several techniques have been developed to detect BPA and nitrite, including chromatography (Helmke & Duncan, 2007), electrochemiluminescence (Hamed & Li, 2022; Liu, Li, et al., 2024; Niu et al., 2022), spectrophotometry (J. Chen et al., 2021), capillary electrophoresis (Della Betta et al., 2014), and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Hassan et al., 2020). However, some of these methods are not suitable for the rapid detection of BPA and nitrite due to high costs, expensive equipment, and the need for highly skilled personnel. Therefore, the development of a simple, rapid, and cost-effective analytical technique for detecting BPA and nitrite is of paramount importance.

Chemiluminescence (CL) is a phenomenon involving light emission through chemical reactions, energy transfer, and photodissociation processes (H. Chen et al., 2014; L. Zhao et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2020). Compared with other analytical techniques, CL offers high sensitivity, has a rapid response, and does not depend on an external light source (Deshpande & Kanungo, 2023), making it one of among the most effective analytical methods available. Moreover, the integration of flow injection (FI) analysis with CL has led to enhanced analytical performance. Consequently, flow injection chemiluminescence (FI-CL) technology is widely applied in clinical diagnostics, food safety (Al Mughairy & Al-Lawati, 2020), and environmental monitoring (Zhu et al., 2023). Carbon Dots (CDs) are a novel class of luminescent, zero-dimensional nanomaterials with particle sizes ranging from 1 to 10 nm, and are characterized by their fluorescent properties (Giordano et al., 2023). CDs consist of a carbon core surrounded by functional groups such as CO, OH, and COOH (Baker & Baker, 2010; Cheng et al., 2021). Compared with traditional inorganic semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent molecules, CDs offer advantages such as low toxicity, excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility (Y. Q. Dong et al., 2012), simple synthesis (Liu, Zhong, et al., 2024), and eco-friendliness (Kanwal et al., 2022). Since their discovery in 2004, extensive research has been conducted to improve their physicochemical properties and expand their applications in various fields (Jiang et al., 2018; J. J. Liu et al., 2021; Y. Yan et al., 2019). Owing to their exceptional properties, CDs have attracted significant attention from researchers (Y. Dong et al., 2015). Various types of CDs have been incorporated into different CL systems to enhance CL sensitivity and stability. Researchers have reported that the reasons for the enhancement of different CL systems by CDs can be broadly divided into three categories: catalysts (Shen et al., 2020), chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) receptors and direct involvement in oxidation reactions (Yongcun Yan et al., 2020). For instance, Zhao et al. (2024) synthesized N,S-CDs from Artemisia annua through a one-step hydrothermal method and successfully utilized N,S-CDs in an acid-KMnO4 CL system for detecting sulfonamides in chicken samples. Similarly, Yang et al. (2022) synthesized Cl, P co-doped CDs (CDs-Cl, P) that significantly enhanced the KMnO4 + H2O2 CL system and successfully applied them for detecting Cu2+ and tannic acid in different samples.

In this study, Cl/CDs were synthesized using ascorbic acid (AA) as the carbon source and hydrochloric acid as the Cl dopant. The Cl/CDs were characterized by fluorescence (FL), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), and other techniques. The synthesized Cl/CDs were rich in surface functional groups, exhibited excellent optical properties, and had good dispersibility. Introduction Cl/CDs into the acid-KMnO4 system produces a strong CL signal was produced, resulting in an amplification of the original CL intensity by approximately 50 times. However, the CL intensity of the new system decreased with increasing concentrations of BPA and nitrite. These findings suggest that BPA and nitrite have inhibitory effects on the Cl/CDs + KMnO4 + HCHO system. Based on this inhibitory phenomenon, a novel CL sensor was developed for the rapid detection of BPA and nitrite.

The degree of defects in Cl/CDs was characterized by Raman spectroscopy using a Renishaw in Via Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, UK). The CL signal was measured by an MPI-B CL analyser, specifically the IFIS-D Intelligent Flow Injection Sampler for FI (Xi'an Remax Electronic Technology Co., Ltd.). FL and CL spectra were collected using an FS5C fluorescence spectrometer (Shanghai Tianmei Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd). The Cl/CDs were synthesized in an electrically heated blast drying oven (GZX-9246MBE).

Ham, pickles, celery and bottled water samples were purchased from a local supermarket. Lake water was collected from Sanchun Lake at the University Town campus of Chongqing Normal University, and tap water was obtained from the laboratory. KMnO4, formaldehyde (HCHO), nitrite and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. BPA, AA, PBS buffer, nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT) and thiourea (TU) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment. For all injected solutions, BPA, nitrite and KMnO4 were prepared using a solid reagent configuration of 1 mM. A 10 % HCHO solution was prepared using a 40 % HCHO solution, and a 2 M HCl solution was prepared using a 36 % HCl solution. All solutions were diluted stepwise at the time of use.

The operational structure of the FI-CL unit is shown in Fig. S1. The Cl/CDs solution, HCl solution and HCHO solution enter the flow path through the main pump and are mixed through a six-way valve, while the KMnO4 and the sample to be tested, and the carrier solution entered the flow path under the pressure of the secondary pump and are mixed through another three-way valve. All solutions were mixed at the last three-way valve and enter the spiral glass flow cell to produce the CL reaction. The CL signal was received by the PMT and imported into the computer for data acquisition (PMT high voltage 650 V, amplification level 4). The length of the injection time is manually controlled to assess the stability of the reaction and the speed of return to baseline. The effect of BPA and nitrite on relative CL intensity is measured as ΔX, where ΔX = X
0 − X
1 , with X
1 and X
0 being the CL signals in the presence and absence of BPA and nitrite, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the preparation method for Cl/CDs was modified based on the literature (Yang et al., 2022). Specifically, 1 g of AA was weighed and dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water, followed by 10 min of ultrasonication. After sonication, 1 mL of HCl was added and transferred to a stainless-steel reaction kettle. The reaction kettle was placed in an electrically heated blast drying oven and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. At the end of the reaction, the reactor was removed and cooled to room temperature. The mixture was subsequently centrifuged at 13,000 r/min for 10 min, after which the supernatant was collected. The supernatant was filtered several times through a 0.22 μm membrane and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (Mw = 500–1000) for 36 h. The resulting solution is referred to as Cl/CDs.

Lake water, bottled water and tap water were tested for BPA. The bottled water and tap water were used without any pre-treatment, while the lake water was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane to remove large particles and impurities. Subsequently, the filtered lake water was then diluted by a factor of two with PBS (pH = 7) and used as the sample to be measured.

To extract nitrite from pickles, ham and celery, 5 g of each sample was weighed and added to 80 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min, with intermittent shaking every 5 min to ensure better processing. After sonication, the solution was heated in a water bath at 75 °C for 5 min and then cooled to room temperature. The final solution was centrifuged at 11,000 r/min for 15 min and the supernatant was collected. The supernatant was filtered several times through a 0.45 μm membrane to remove insoluble substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of the KMnO4 + HCHO system by Cl/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. S2, CL curves were measured for the Cl/CDs + KMnO4 (i), KMnO4 + HCHO (ii), and Cl/CDs + KMnO4 + HCHO (iii) systems. In an acidic medium, the oxidation of HCHO by KMnO4 produces a relatively weak CL signal. The addition of Cl/CDs increased the CL intensity of the acid-KMnO4 system, indicating that Cl/CDs have a good sensitizing effect on the acid-KMnO4 system. Additionally, the introduction of a solution containing the same concentration of raw materials used to prepare Cl/CDs into the KMnO4 + HCHO system resulted in no enhancement effect, confirming that the CL enhancement is due to the presence of Cl/CDs. In CL system, the method of mixing different reagents effects on the CL intensity of the designed CL system. The effects of different methods of mixing solutions on CL were investigated, as shown in Table S1. The maximum CL intensity was observed when KMnO4 was injected into a mixed solution of HCHO, Cl/CDs, and H+. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S3, the effects of synthesis time and temperature of Cl/CDs on the CL intensity of the system were compared. It was found that the maximum enhancement of CL intensity in the acid-KMnO4 system occurred when the Cl/CDs were synthesized at 180 °C for 4 h, with the enhancement being more than 50-fold. Therefore, the Cl/CDs prepared under the conditions of heating at 180 °C for 4 h were selected for the experimental.

As shown in the TEM images in Fig. 2 , the synthesized Cl/CDs exhibited a regular spherical structure with good dispersion, and the particle sizes of the Cl/CDs ranged from 0.5 to 5 nm, with an average diameter of 2.17 nm (Fig. 2a, inset). HR-TEM images (Fig. 2b, inset) reveal that the crystal structure of Cl/CDs closely resembles the (100) plane of graphite, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Y. Dong et al., 2010). The Raman spectrum of the Cl/CDs (Fig. 3a) displays two characteristic peaks at 1245 cm−1 and 1456 cm−1, corresponding to the disordered D band (sp3-hybridization) and the crystalline G band (sp2-hybridization), respectively. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (I D/I G) is 0.935, indicating a pronounced graphite structure in the Cl/CDs. The XRD pattern (Fig. 3b) shows a broader diffraction peak of the Cl/CDs at 2θ = 22°, with a peak value similar to that of the (002) plane of graphite (Sharma & Das, 2019), indicating an ordered crystal structure and high crystallinity of Cl/CDs.

The FTIR spectrum of the Cl/CDs is shown in Fig. 3c. The absorption peaks at 3405 cm−1 and 2934 cm−1 are attributed to the CH and OH bonds, while peaks at 1707 cm−1 and 1636 cm−1 are due to CO bonds. The characteristic peaks at 1404 cm−1, 1151 cm−1, and 1024 cm−1 correspond to the CC, CO, and COC bonds, respectively, and the peak at 673 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of CCl bonds. The elemental composition of Cl/CDs was investigated via XPS (Fig. 3d), and the XPS spectrum revealed characteristic peaks at 532.2 eV for O 1 s, 284.8 eV for C 1 s, and 199.7 eV for Cl 2p, confirming successful Cl doping. The C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 3e shows four distinct peaks: a peak at 284.8 eV corresponding to CC, a peak at 286.6 eV corresponding to CO, a peak at 288.5 eV corresponding to CCl, and a peak at 289.1 eV corresponding to CO. The O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 3f exhibits two peaks at 530.0 eV (CO) and 531.4 eV (CO). The Cl 2p spectrum in Fig. 3g confirms the existence of CCl bonds, with characteristic peaks observed at 195.6 eV (Cl 2p1/2) and 197.3 eV (Cl 2p2/3). The consistent findings from the FTIR and XPS analyses verify the presence of various functional groups in Cl/CDs, which are crucial for the catalytic properties of the CL system.

Fig. 3h illustrates the UV spectrum of Cl/CDs, with a strong absorption peak at 310 nm. The inset on the right side of Fig. 3h demonstrates the bright blue-white emission of Cl/CDs under UV (365 nm) light. Fig. 3i presents the fluorescence spectrum of Cl/CDs, excited with 10 nm increments. The strongest emission is observed around the maximum excitation wavelength of 420 nm within the 350–500 nm range, with the emission peak located at 530 nm. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission peak intensity initially increases and then decreases. This phenomenon may be attributed to the varying surface states and sizes of Cl/CDs affecting their bandgap (Shamsipur et al., 2018). The quantum yield (QY) of Cl/CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference, was 79.44 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for BPA and nitrite detection
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the reaction conditions of the CL sensor for the detection of BPA and nitrite, different solution concentrations of the system and the dilution of the Cl/CDs were investigated, as shown in Fig. S4. The concentration of the synthesized Cl/CDs masterbatch was considered as 1. The optimal dilution concentrations for the detection of nitrite and BPA were when the Cl/CDs were diluted at 30 and 60 times, respectively. The oxidant KMnO4 in the system had a significant effect on the CL intensity, with the greatest response for nitrite and BPA at concentrations of 0.6 mM and 0.9 mM (Fig. S4b). Therefore, 0.6 mM and 0.9 mM of KMnO4 were chosen for subsequent experiments, respectively. HCHO is commonly used for signal amplification in acid-KMnO4 system. The effect of HCHO concentration on CL intensity was evaluated in the range of 1 %–8 % (Fig. S4c). The best response to BPA and nitrite was obtained at HCHO concentrations of 5 % and 7 %, respectively. HCl, which is the reaction environment of the system, had a significant effect on the CL intensity. The highest response strengths for BPA and nitrite were observed at 1 M and 0.7 M HCl concentrations, respectively, after which the response strengths began to decrease. Therefore, 1 M and 0.7 M HCl were selected as the optimal conditions for detecting BPA and nitrite (Fig. S4d). In conclusion, the final conditions for the detection of nitrite were 0.6 mM KMnO4, 0.7 M HCl, 7 % HCHO, and a 30-fold dilution of Cl/CDs; and the final conditions for detecting BPA were 0.9 mM KMnO4, 1 M HCl, 5 % HCHO, and 60-fold dilution of Cl/CDs.

The selectivity of the designed CL sensor was assessed (Fig. 4a&b), investigating the impact of BPA analogues, nitrite analogues, and competing ions on CL intensity. The findings revealed that BPA and nitrite exerted significant inhibitory effects on the system at low concentrations, while other compounds had negligible effects. Based on these inhibitory effects, a highly sensitive and rapid CL sensor was developed for the detection of BPA and nitrite. A series of BPA and nitrite concentrations were tested under optimal conditions, with results shown in Fig. 4c&d. The CL intensity of the Cl/CDs-CL sensor decreased as the concentrations of BPA and nitrite increased. The linear ranges for BPA and nitrite determined as 0.05–10 μM and 0.7–100 μM, respectively (Fig. 4e&f). The regression equations were ΔX = 82.31x + 344.26 (R2 = 0.9949) for BPA and ΔX = 11.679x + 369.16 (R2 = 0.9961) for nitrite, where ΔX represents the difference in CL intensity in the presence and absence of BPA and nitrite in the Cl/CDs-KMnO4 system, and where x represents the BPA (0.1 μM) and nitrite (1 μM) concentrations. The LODs for BPA and nitrite were 0.86 nM and 22.5 nM, respectively, and the LOQs were 2.8 nM and 75 nM (LOD = 3 × SD/K, LOQ = 10 × SD/K). Eleven repeated measurements of 0.1 μM BPA and 1 μM nitrite yielded intraday relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 2.3 % and 1.4 % (n = 11), respectively. Inter-day RSDs for analyses of BPA (0.1 μM) and nitrite (1 μM) over 3 consecutive days were at 1.3 % to 2.7 % and 1.2 % to 2.9 %, respectively. Table 1 shows the LOD and linear range results for the detection of BPA and nitrite using this method in comparison with other assays. Although some of the methods have wider linear ranges and LODs, the designed method is simpler and more cost-effective to operate.

To identify the emitters generated during the reaction process of the Cl/CDs-CL sensor, the CL spectra of the relevant systems were investigated utilizing a FL spectrophotometer. As illustrated in Fig. S5a, two emission peaks were observed in the Cl/CDs + KMnO4 + HCHO sensor (curve 2). The peak at 480 nm corresponds to the FL spectrum of Cl/CDs excited at 380 nm, while the peak at 620 nm corresponds to the CL spectrum of another luminescent species Mn (II). Consequently, it is hypothesized that two emitting species, Mn (II) and Cl/CDs, are present during the reaction process. The UV spectrum depicted in Fig. S5b shows two absorption peaks at ∼350 nm and 530 nm in the KMnO4 + HCHO solution (curve I). The KMnO4 peak disappeared after the addition of Cl/CDs (curve II), and the solution rapidly turned colorless, indicating that Cl/CDs facilitate the oxidation of KMnO4. In the acid-KMnO4 system, the reaction between KMnO4 and HCl yields an intermediate product, permanganic acid ester, which decomposes to produce Mn (III). With the addition of Cl/CDs, the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of Cl/CDs react with the permanganic acid ester, consequently accelerating the generation of Mn (III) (Z. Wang et al., 2024) and thereby increasing the CL intensity.

Different radical scavengers were added to the Cl/CDs-CL sensor to investigate the mechanism behind Cl/CDs* luminescent species generation and to determine if intermediate species formed during the CL reaction process are involved. AA is commonly used as a scavenger for various reactive oxygen species and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Adding 0.1 mM AA resulted in almost complete quenching of the CL intensity of the Cl/CDs sensor, suggesting that the intermediate radical species generated during the reaction are crucial. Thiourea (TU) is a typical scavenger for •OH radicals (Whiteman & Halliwell, 1997). The addition of 0.1 mM TU led to approximately 30 % inhibition of the CL intensity. NBT acts as a scavenger for superoxide anions (•O2 −) (Yamakoshi et al., 2003). When 0.1 mM NBT was added, the CL intensity decreased by approximately 37 %. Interestingly, as these intermediate species do not directly emit light, it is suggested that the generation of luminescent species in Cl/CDs* involves the reduction of •O2 − and the oxidation of •OH radicals injecting electrons and holes into Cl/CDs, forming Cl/CDs•+ and Cl/CDs•–, followed by electron transfer annihilation, formation of excited state Cl/CDs*. Based on these results, a proposed reaction mechanism for the Cl/CDs-CL sensor is illustrated in Scheme 1.

Upon the addition of BPA and nitrite, the CL intensity of the system is inhibited. As shown in Fig. S5a (curve 3), after the addition of BPA, the peak of Mn (II)* in the CL spectrum disappears beyond 600 nm, while the intensity of the peak at 480 nm corresponding to Cl/CDs* decreases. This is because when BPA is added, the reaction between KMnO4 and BPA reduces the oxidizing agent in the system, leading to a decrease in the CL intensity of the system. The UV spectrum (Fig. S5b, curve III) shows that upon mixing BPA with KMnO4, the absorption peak of KMnO4 disappears (X. Chen et al., 2011; Z. Y. Dong et al., 2022), and the solution quickly fades upon mixing BPA with KMnO4, further confirming the reaction between BPA and KMnO4.

As shown in the CL spectrum in Fig. S5a (curve 4), there is no change in the luminescent species of the system upon the addition of nitrite. However, the intensity of the peaks after 480 nm and 600 nm decreases. This is because nitrite reacts with the carbonyl functional groups on the surface of Cl/CDs under acidic conditions, forming new adducts (Pan et al., 2024), which alters the properties of Cl/CDs, reduces their catalytic ability in the Cl/CDs-CL sensor, and consequently decreases the luminescent species Cl/CDs* and Mn (II)*. In the UV spectrum (Fig. S5b), the absorption peak of KMnO4 still exists in the mixture solution of KMnO4 and nitrite (curve IV), while the absorption peak of Cl/CDs is slightly reduced in the mixture solution of Cl/CDs and nitrite (curve V). The Cl/CDs was reduced by NaBH4 (NaBH4 can selectively reduce the carbonyl functional groups on the nanomaterials to hydroxyl functional groups and does not affect the structure of other functional groups). After the reduction, the solution was dialyzed for 24 h after the end of the reduction in order to remove the excess NaBH4. The reduced solution was named as r-Cl/CDs, and the Cl/CDs in the system were replaced with r-Cl/CDs. The inhibition of the r-Cl/CDs-KMnO4-H+ system by nitrite was significantly reduced, and the above results further confirmed that the addition of nitrite to the Cl/CDs-KMnO4-H+ system resulted in the reaction between nitrite and Cl/CDs leading to the reduction of CL intensity.

In the detection process, itself, certain substances found in food and water environments can cause errors in the experimental results. To assess the anti-interference capability of the Cl/CDs-KMnO4 CL sensor for detecting BPA and nitrite, without interference, various potential interfering substances were added to 1 μM solutions of BPA and nitrite, and the CL intensity was measured. Table 2 shows the acceptable concentration ratios for these interfering substances, where a relative error of <5 % was observed. These results demonstrate the strong anti-interference capability and stability of the CL sensor.

As shown in Table 3 , to evaluate the detection sensitivity of the developed CL sensor for BPA and nitrite in real samples, the levels of BPA in tap water, bottled water, and lake water, as well as the levels of nitrite in pickles, ham, and celery were determined. To assess the accuracy of the CL sensor, the standard addition method was employed to calculate the recovery rates for the actual samples. The recovery rates for BPA and nitrite ranged from 96.3 % to 104.3 % and from 96.3 % to 103.8 %, respectively. Additionally, the accuracy of the developed method was verified by comparing it with the HPLC method. As shown in Table S2, the HPLC method yielded recoveries of BPA and nitrite ranging from 95.7 % to 104.6 % and from 99.8 % to 105.7 %, respectively. Student t-test was conducted to compare the two methods, and the results indicated no statistically significant difference between the CL sensor and HPLC method (p = 0.05). These results indicate that the developed CL sensor demonstrates high accuracy in detecting BPA and nitrite in real samples.

In this study, Cl/CDs with favourable optical properties were synthesized using the hydrothermal method, employing AA and HCl as precursor materials. Cl/CDs significantly enhanced the CL intensity of the acid-KMnO4 system. The effects of different synthesis temperatures and durations on the CL intensity of the acid-KMnO4 system were investigated to determine the optimal synthesis conditions, leading to the development of a novel CL sensor. The luminophores in the system were identified as Cl/CDs* and Mn (II)* through CL spectra, free radical scavenging experiments, and UV absorbance measurements. The sensor demonstrated significant inhibition in the presence of BPA and nitrite, whereby the decrease in CL intensity was directly proportional to the concentrations of BPA and nitrite over a certain linear range. Additionally, the sensor exhibited excellent selectivity and immunity to interference. Based on these findings, a highly sensitive and user-friendly CL sensor was designed and successfully utilized for the analysis of BPA and nitrite in various samples.

Yujie Xiao: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Junjie Mu: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Qiuxiong Chen: Methodology. Yuxian Mai: Methodology, Data curation. Ying Li: Methodology, Investigation. Hao Xue: Methodology. Pengyang Song: Resources. Xian Yang: Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Hong Zheng: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the Science and Technology Research Program of the Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (Grant No. KJZD-K202300504) and the Innovation and Central Nervous System Drug Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (Grant No. 230041-01SZ). Thanks for the financial support from the project ‘Wildlife Resource Survey of Montenegro Nature Reserve in Wansheng’.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.141374.
